_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Ashtabula County, OH. Organized 1808 from Trumbull and Geauga counties. Record survival is excellent. The county's intense Underground Railroad activity left oblique documentary traces — deed records showing property transfers to known conductors, church records showing community mobilization.

previous_courthouses.fate: Early courthouse. No significant record loss.

previous_courthouses.location: Jefferson, brick courthouse on the square
previous_courthouses.fate: Served through the antebellum era and the Civil War. Joshua Giddings argued abolitionist cases in this building. Replaced by the current courthouse.

record_losses.description: Ashtabula County's courthouse has not experienced a significant fire. Records run continuously from the county's 1808 organization. For earlier records, check Trumbull County. The county's intense Underground Railroad activity did not generate courthouse records — conductors deliberately avoided documentation. However, deed records, tax rolls, and church records from the period can identify known Underground Railroad participants.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The courthouse records survive — but the story of {ancestor_name}'s involvement in the Underground Railroad, if any, will not appear in the county records. The conductors kept no written logs. The evidence is circumstantial: property location, church affiliation, and the testimonies collected after the war by researchers like Wilbur Siebert.
record_losses.source: Ohio History Connection. Guide to County Records in Ohio.

deeds.repository: Ashtabula County Recorder; Ohio History Connection microfilm

wills_estates.repository: Ashtabula County Probate Court; Ohio History Connection

marriages.repository: Ashtabula County Probate Court; Ohio History Connection

vital_records.completeness: Ohio statewide; compliance poor until approximately 1908.

substitute_sources.source_type: Underground Railroad testimonies and Siebert collection
substitute_sources.description: Professor Wilbur Siebert of Ohio State University collected extensive oral testimonies and correspondence from Underground Railroad participants and their descendants in the 1890s-1900s. Ashtabula County is one of the most heavily documented counties in his collection.
substitute_sources.repository: Ohio History Connection (Siebert Collection); Ashtabula County Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Names specific conductors, stations, and routes. Identifies families who participated. Does not document the fugitives themselves (they used false names), but documents the community network that aided them.
substitute_sources.source: Siebert, Wilbur H. The Underground Railroad from Slavery to Freedom. New York: Macmillan, 1898.

substitute_sources.description: Ashtabula County's nineteen surviving covered bridges have construction records, county commissioner minutes authorizing construction, and engineering specifications that document the built landscape. Bridge records name builders, commissioners, and sometimes road petition signatories.
substitute_sources.repository: Ashtabula County Engineer's Office; Ashtabula County Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Road petitions and bridge construction records name residents of specific townships, providing evidence of community participation and residence.

substitute_sources.description: Ashtabula Harbor's iron ore receiving operations employed hundreds of dock workers. The Pittsburgh & Lake Erie Railroad and other operators maintained employment records.
substitute_sources.repository: Ashtabula County Historical Society; Ohio History Connection
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Employment records identify workers at the docks — many of them Finnish, Swedish, and Italian immigrants.

narrative_hooks: Ashtabula County's courthouse records survive from 1808 — {ancestor_name}'s deeds, marriages, and court records are intact.

If {ancestor_name} was an Underground Railroad conductor in Ashtabula County, the evidence is not in the courthouse — it is in the Siebert Collection testimonies, the church records of abolitionist congregations, and the architectural evidence of houses with hidden rooms.

The county's covered bridges outlasted the economy that built them — the construction records at the county engineer's office name the men who petitioned for the bridges and the men who built them, and {ancestor_name} may be among them.
For {ancestor_name} before 1808, the records are in Trumbull County — Ashtabula was carved from the original Reserve county, and the earliest records stayed in Warren.
source_references: Ohio History Connection. Guide to County Records in Ohio. Columbus, 2015.
Large, Moina W. History of Ashtabula County, Ohio. Topeka: Historical Publishing, 1924.
Siebert, Wilbur H. The Underground Railroad from Slavery to Freedom. New York: Macmillan, 1898.
